Name: spectral_precision_routing
Title: Spectral Precision Routing: Training-Free Adaptive Quantization via Geometric Subspace Diagnostics
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states serve as direct proxies for quantization sensitivity, enabling training-free adaptive precision allocation in deep neural networks.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works in spectral quantization routing and geometric signal separation, but differs in its focus on training-free adaptive quantization via geometric subspace diagnostics. Unlike existing methods, our approach does not rely on static profiling or costly calibration, and instead leverages real-time activation geometry to dynamically allocate numerical precision.
Abstract: We propose Spectral Precision Routing (SPR), a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry. By computing local activation covariances and spectral decay rates, we derive a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds that link spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, SPR achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Evaluate SPR on WILDS benchmarks with varying latency budgets and precision levels.
Experiment 2: Compare SPR with state-of-the-art mixed-precision methods on single-GPU setups.
Experiment 3: Investigate the effect of spectral decay rate and covariance condition number on quantization sensitivity and precision allocation.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed method may be sensitive to hyperparameter tuning and require careful calibration of precision-switching thresholds.
The use of real-time activation geometry may introduce additional computational overhead, which could impact latency and throughput.
